nodes.name: Body Position
nodes.description: Body position is the foundation for all other backstroke techniques. A flat, horizontal body with hips riding high reduces drag and improves efficiency. This is the easiest technique to master and serves as the base upon which everything else is built.

nodes.name: Head Position
nodes.description: Head position in backstroke is critical because it directly controls body and hip position. The head should rest with the water line at ear level, looking straight up at the ceiling or sky. A stable, neutral head position keeps the hips high and maintains a streamlined body line.

nodes.name: Flutter Kick
nodes.description: The flutter kick in backstroke is a hip-driven, continuous motion that provides propulsion and helps maintain body position. A compact kick with relaxed ankles and proper rhythm is essential for both sprint and distance backstroke events.

nodes.name: Breathing
nodes.description: Breathing in backstroke is fundamentally different from other strokes because the face is always above water. Natural, rhythmic breathing without breath-holding helps maintain relaxation and efficient stroke mechanics throughout the race.

nodes.name: Body Rotation
nodes.description: Body rotation in backstroke involves rolling 30-45 degrees (research range: 30-60 degrees) around the spine axis. Shoulders and hips rotate together as a unit, which extends arm reach, adds pull power, and reduces frontal drag compared to swimming flat.

nodes.name: Arm Stroke Entry
nodes.description: Arm stroke entry in backstroke sets up the entire pull phase. The pinky finger enters first at slightly outside shoulder width with the arm fully extended. A clean, pinky-first entry with minimal splash ensures proper catch position and avoids creating unnecessary drag.

nodes.name: Arm Stroke Recovery
nodes.description: The arm recovery in backstroke uses a bent-arm motion where the elbow leads and the hand trails below. The thumb exits the water first, the hand rotates during recovery, and the pinky leads entry. A relaxed recovery path close to the body line is efficient and conserves energy.

nodes.name: Arm Stroke Catch
nodes.description: The catch is the initial phase where the hand anchors on the water immediately after entry. A high-elbow, early vertical forearm (EVF) position creates maximum leverage by using the entire forearm as a propulsive surface. This is one of the most impactful technique improvements a swimmer can make.

nodes.name: Arm Stroke Pull/Push
nodes.description: The pull/push phase is where most propulsion is generated in backstroke. A relatively straight hand path (with minor lateral adjustments of 5-15cm) combined with high elbow and acceleration through to the hip finish creates maximum forward thrust. Pull contributes approximately 70-85% of race propulsion in sprint events.

nodes.name: Stroke Timing & Coordination
nodes.description: Stroke timing and coordination integrate all other backstroke techniques into a unified, continuous motion. Arms move in opposition (when one enters, the other pulls), the kick rhythm synchronizes with the stroke cycle, and body rotation peaks at entry. Mastering timing is what separates good swimmers from great ones.

nodes.name: Starts and Turns
nodes.description: Starts and turns are the most technically complex elements of backstroke. They involve launching backward from the wall, executing underwater dolphin kicks within the 15m limit, counting strokes to know when to rotate for the flip turn, and pushing off in a tight streamline. Mastery of starts and turns provides significant time advantages, particularly in short course pools.
